findings.title: Postgres connection string stored in plaintext ConfigMap
findings.description: Subtask 1007 constructs the ConfigMap `sigma-1-infra-endpoints` containing `CNPG_SIGMA1_PG_URL` which is a full Postgres connection URI including credentials (username, password, host, port, dbname). ConfigMaps are not encrypted at rest by default in Kubernetes and are readable by anyone with RBAC read access to ConfigMaps in the namespace. This effectively downgrades the credential from a Secret to a ConfigMap.
findings.recommendation: Store the Postgres connection URI in a Kubernetes Secret instead of a ConfigMap. Reference the CNPG-generated app secret directly in pod specs via `secretRef` in `envFrom`, or create a composite Secret that aggregates all sensitive connection strings. Keep the ConfigMap for non-sensitive values only (Redis URL without auth, bridge service URLs).

findings.title: No authentication on PM server API endpoints
findings.description: Tasks 2 and 3 add multiple API endpoints (POST /api/pipeline/delegate-status, POST /api/pipeline/trigger, GET /api/pipeline/status, GET /api/pipeline/runs/{runId}/*) but there is no explicit task or subtask for implementing authentication or authorization on these endpoints. The delegate-status endpoint exposes internal agent-to-user-ID mappings. The pipeline trigger endpoint could be abused to create unauthorized Linear issues and GitHub PRs. Task 4001 mentions 'ensure the route is protected by any existing auth guards' but this is vague and not validated.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit subtask to implement API authentication on all PM server endpoints. At minimum, require the SERVICE_API_KEY in an Authorization header for all API calls. Add middleware-level auth validation in the Elysia router. Include a test case that verifies unauthenticated requests receive 401 responses.

findings.title: No input validation on pipeline trigger PRD payload
findings.description: Task 6 submits a PRD payload to POST /api/pipeline/trigger, and Task 2 processes this PRD to create Linear issues and GitHub PRs. There is no task or subtask addressing input validation/sanitization of the PRD content. Malicious or malformed PRD content could result in injection into Linear issue descriptions (XSS via markdown), GitHub PR bodies, or the idempotency key computation (hash collision attacks). The idempotency key in Task 2003 embeds content into Linear issue descriptions as HTML comments, which could be escaped.
findings.recommendation: Add input validation for the PRD payload: enforce maximum size limits, validate schema structure, and sanitize any user-supplied text before embedding it into Linear issue descriptions or GitHub PR bodies. Ensure the HTML comment idempotency marker cannot be spoofed by PRD content containing `-->` sequences.

findings.title: SERVICE_API_KEY is a single shared static key for all service-to-service auth
findings.description: Task 1 provisions a single `SERVICE_API_KEY` used for authenticating the PM server to both Discord and Linear bridge services (Tasks 5002, 5003). A single shared static key means compromise of any one service exposes all bridge endpoints. There is no mention of key rotation for this specific key (external-secrets refreshInterval in Task 10006 covers the backing store, but the bridge services must also be updated).
findings.recommendation: Consider using per-service API keys (e.g., DISCORD_SERVICE_KEY, LINEAR_SERVICE_KEY) to limit blast radius of a single key compromise. Ensure bridge services are included in the secret rotation flow from Task 10006 and can handle key rotation gracefully (accept old and new keys during a transition window).

findings.title: Structured logs may leak sensitive data
findings.description: Task 2007 standardizes structured JSON logging across all pipeline stages. Multiple subtasks log payload details including agent hints, Linear user IDs, idempotency keys, error messages from API responses, and retry details. There is no mention of log scrubbing or ensuring that API tokens, webhook URLs, or PII are never included in log entries. Error messages from the Linear or Hermes APIs could contain token fragments or sensitive user data.
findings.recommendation: Add a log sanitization layer in the logger module (Task 2007) that redacts known sensitive patterns (API keys, Bearer tokens, webhook URLs) from log output. Establish a logging policy that error messages from external APIs are truncated and sanitized before logging. Never log the full HTTP request/response bodies for external API calls.

findings.title: Redis deployed without authentication
findings.description: Subtask 1003 deploys Redis as a bare Deployment with `redis:7-alpine` image and no authentication configured (no `requirepass`, no ACLs). Any pod that can reach Redis on port 6379 can read/write all data. While Cilium network policies (Task 1008) restrict access, defense-in-depth requires Redis authentication as a secondary control.
findings.recommendation: Configure Redis with a password (`requirepass`) stored as a Kubernetes Secret via ExternalSecrets. Update the Redis connection string to include the password. For the HA configuration in Task 10002, ensure Sentinel also uses authentication (`sentinel auth-pass`).

findings.title: No Pod Security Standards enforcement
findings.description: Task 1001 creates the namespace with resource quotas and limit ranges but does not apply Pod Security Standards (PSS) or Pod Security Admission (PSA) labels. Without these, containers can run as root, use privileged mode, or mount the host filesystem. The smoke test pod (Task 1009) and Redis deployment use generic images without explicit security contexts.
findings.recommendation: Apply Pod Security Admission labels to the namespace: `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted` (or at minimum `baseline`). Add explicit `securityContext` to all pod specs: `runAsNonRoot: true`, `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`, `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`, `capabilities: {drop: ['ALL']}`. Use specific non-root UIDs for Redis and smoke test containers.

findings.title: Container image tags not pinned to digests
findings.description: Subtask 1003 specifies `redis:7-alpine` as the image tag for Redis. Mutable tags can be overwritten in the registry (tag mutation attack). Other pods (smoke test, PM server) also likely use mutable tags. No task addresses container image scanning or digest pinning.
findings.recommendation: Pin all container images to specific SHA256 digests (e.g., `redis@sha256:abc123...`) rather than mutable tags. Add a subtask for container image vulnerability scanning using Trivy or similar before deployment. Consider using an admission controller (e.g., Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) to enforce digest-only image references.

findings.title: No TLS for in-cluster database and cache connections
findings.description: The Postgres and Redis connections from the PM server use unencrypted in-cluster connections (no `sslmode=require` in Postgres URI, no TLS for Redis). While Cilium provides network-level encryption options, the connection strings in Task 1007 don't enforce TLS. An attacker with access to the network namespace could sniff credentials and data.
findings.recommendation: Enable TLS on the CloudNative-PG cluster (CNPG supports automatic TLS certificate management) and set `sslmode=require` or `sslmode=verify-full` in the connection string. For Redis, enable TLS using the `--tls-port` and `--tls-cert-file` configuration options. This provides defense-in-depth beyond network policies.

findings.title: Delegate mapping endpoint lacks access control differentiation
findings.description: Task 2006 creates `POST /api/pipeline/delegate-status` that exposes the full agent-to-Linear-user-ID mapping. This is described as an observability endpoint but uses POST method (unusual for read-only) and has no differentiated access control. It could be used for reconnaissance to identify which Linear users are associated with which agent roles.
findings.recommendation: Change the endpoint to GET (it's a read-only query). Apply rate limiting. Consider restricting access to this endpoint to internal/admin consumers only, or redact the full Linear user IDs in the response (e.g., show only the last 4 characters).

findings.title: Third-party circuit breaker library selection needs Bun compatibility vetting
findings.description: Task 3 identifies a decision point about using `opossum` vs `cockatiel` vs a custom implementation for the circuit breaker. The task correctly notes opossum's Node.js EventEmitter dependency may not work under Bun. However, there's no mention of auditing the chosen library for known vulnerabilities before adoption.
findings.recommendation: Whichever library is selected, run `npm audit` and check Snyk/GitHub Advisory Database for known vulnerabilities. If implementing a custom circuit breaker, ensure it undergoes code review for timing attacks and state race conditions in the half-open state.

findings.title: No alerting for security-relevant events
findings.description: While Task 10007 adds audit logging, there is no task for setting up alerts on security-relevant events such as: repeated authentication failures on bridge endpoints, circuit breaker state changes indicating external API compromise, secret rotation failures, network policy violations, or unauthorized access attempts to the PM server API.
findings.recommendation: Add a subtask under Task 10 to configure alerting rules (via Prometheus AlertManager or the cluster's alerting system) for: ExternalSecret sync failures, repeated 401/403 responses from bridge services, Cilium policy deny events exceeding a threshold, and circuit breaker state transitions to 'open'. These should notify the operations team via the existing Discord webhook.

findings.title: Linear and GitHub API tokens have no scope restrictions documented
findings.description: Tasks 1004 and 1005 provision `LINEAR_API_TOKEN` and `GITHUB_API_TOKEN` via ExternalSecrets, but there is no documentation or validation of the minimum required scopes/permissions for these tokens. An overly permissive GitHub token (e.g., with `repo:delete` or `admin:org` scope) or Linear token could be exploited if compromised. The PM server only needs to create issues and PRs, not admin-level access.
findings.recommendation: Document the minimum required scopes for each API token: GitHub token should be limited to `repo` scope (or fine-grained with only `contents:write` and `pull_requests:write` on the specific repository). Linear token should be scoped to the specific team/workspace with issue creation permissions only. Add a validation step in the smoke test or startup that verifies token permissions match expected scope.

strengths: Strong network segmentation with Cilium NetworkPolicies implementing deny-all defaults and explicit allowlisting per service path (Tasks 1008, 10004)
External secrets management via external-secrets operator with ExternalSecret CRDs, avoiding hardcoded secrets in manifests (Tasks 1004, 1005)
Least-privilege RBAC with a dedicated ServiceAccount for the PM server restricted to read-only ConfigMap and Secret access (Task 1006, 10005)
Circuit breaker pattern for external API calls prevents cascading failures and limits exposure surface to external services (Task 3002)
Graceful degradation throughout the pipeline — notification failures, Hermes unavailability, and delegate resolution failures do not block pipeline completion, reducing denial-of-service impact
Secret rotation planning with configurable refreshInterval and rolling restart triggers (Task 10006)
Comprehensive production hardening task (Task 10) addressing HA, TLS, network policies, RBAC, secret rotation, and audit logging as a dedicated phase
summary: The plan demonstrates solid security foundations with network segmentation, external secret management, least-privilege RBAC, and a dedicated production hardening phase. However, three high-severity findings require attention: the Postgres connection string stored in a plaintext ConfigMap (SEC-001), missing authentication on PM server API endpoints (SEC-002), and undocumented API token scope restrictions (SEC-013). Medium-severity concerns include Redis running without authentication, lack of Pod Security Standards enforcement, potential sensitive data in structured logs, and a shared static service-to-service API key. Addressing these findings before production deployment would bring the overall risk level from medium to low.
